I want to be straight with you: we came in below plan.
We missed the target, and I take responsibility for it.
We will close the shortfall by quarter end.
We are meeting the supplier on Thursday to course-correct.
If the pilot slips again, we will pull the stopgap budget.
Victor, thanks for calling. I have a board update tomorrow, are we on track for the quarter?
That is exactly why I am calling. There is no easy way to say this: we came in below plan, with a shortfall of about ten percent.
That is a real setback. What happened, and who dropped the ball?
Honestly, we missed the target, and I own that fully. The root cause was a late forecast on my side, not the team effort. I am sorry it landed on you the day before your board.
I appreciate the honesty. But the board will ask me how we fix it. What is the plan?
Two things. We will close the shortfall by quarter end, and we are meeting the supplier on Thursday to course-correct the pipeline.
And if Thursday does not land? I need to know the downside is covered.
It is. If the supplier slips again, we will pull the stopgap budget and protect your core accounts first. I will send you a one-page summary tonight so you can head off any surprises at the board.
That works. A bad quarter I can defend; being blindsided I can not. Send the summary.
We had a ten percent shortfall, and the accountability is mine.
I named the root cause and reassured them we will course-correct this week.
There is no easy way to say this: we came in below plan.

Okay, the numbers are in and they are not good, so I have to call Beatriz before the board does. There is no easy way to say this, and pretending otherwise only makes it worse. My plan is simple. First, I lead in honestly: I want to be straight with you, the quarter came in below plan. No burying it on slide nine. Second, I own it in active voice. We missed the target. Not mistakes were made, not the market moved against us. We missed it, and I take responsibility for the call that got us here. Over-apologizing helps no one, so one clear sorry, then forward. Third, I reassure with a real plan, not vague comfort. I name the root cause, I say what we are going to do this week, and I make a firm promise with will: we will close the shortfall by quarter end. And I add a contingency: if the pilot slips again, we will pull the stopgap budget and protect the core accounts. Honest about the past, concrete about the future. That is how you keep trust when the news is bad.

Hi Victor, it is Mariana. A quick note before you deliver this. Bad news is not the enemy; a clumsy delivery is. Three things to hold onto. First, get ahead of it. Do not let stakeholders hear the setback from someone else. A short, honest lead-in earns you the room: there is no easy way to say this, or, I want to be straight with you. Second, own it cleanly and move on. Say we missed the target, in active voice, and resist the passive blame-shifting that everyone sees through. Take responsibility, apologize once, and do not grovel; over-apologizing makes you look shaky, not sincere. Third, and this is the one people skip, reassure with a concrete plan, not feelings. Name the root cause, commit with will to what you will fix, lay out what you are going to do this week, and add a contingency with if: if the supplier slips, we will switch to the backup. People can forgive a bad quarter. What they cannot forgive is feeling misled or left without a plan. Be transparent, be accountable, and give them the next step. You have got this.
